summary.intent: Debug why browser screencast frames were not reaching the WebSocket/JS client in the gateway service layer, despite CDP frames being produced correctly.
summary.outcome: Root cause identified and fixed: 'browser.screencast.frame' was missing from the WebSocket subscription list in websocket.js, causing the gateway to silently drop all screencast frames to the client.

repo: The gateway broadcast infrastructure uses a subscription filter at broadcast.rs:100-102 — clients must explicitly subscribe to event types or have subscriptions=None (wildcard).

The WebSocket client has two separate event maps: eventHandlers (used to build subscription list via Object.keys) and eventListeners (used by onEvent). Screencast events must be added to eventHandlers to be subscribed.
Sandbox mode uses browserless/chrome via apple-container VM isolation; host mode uses detected local browsers (chrome, edge, brave).

code.finding: Subscription filter: clients without a matching subscription silently skip the event. subscriptions=None means wildcard (all events pass through).
'browser.screencast.frame' was absent from the subscription list built from Object.keys(eventHandlers), causing the gateway to never deliver screencast frames to this client.

workflow: Layered trace logging (subscribe_screencast, screencast relay, first frame received) was effective for binary-searching the broken link in a multi-hop pipeline.
Integration tests passing in host mode but failing in sandbox mode can indicate environment-specific subscription or relay wiring differences rather than core logic bugs.

friction: Multiple redundant grep attempts for 'subscribeEvents' suggest the symbol name was uncertain or results were ambiguous — required several retries to locate the right file.
The gateway silently drops frames for unsubscribed events with no warning log, making the failure invisible without tracing added upstream.
open_items: Verify the JS fix works end-to-end in sandbox mode with actual frame rendering in the browser UI.
Consider adding a warning log in the gateway broadcast path when an event is dropped due to no matching subscribers, to make future misconfigurations easier to diagnose.
